text: The surgeon begins the operation by using sharp scissors to dissect and elevate the left lobe of the liver from the gastric bypass anatomy. The dissection focuses on preserving the gastric junal anastomosis for later stages. The surgeon then separates the rhulim along its length down to the transverse colon using a combination of hot and cold scissors, ensuring the integrity of the proximal rule limb for future reconstruction. Adhesions are carefully dissected on both sides.

The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery and divides the proximal Roux limb, using a vessel sealer to transect the blood supply and facilitate demarcation. Omental adhesions are carefully taken down throughout the patient's prior gastric bypass anatomy. The vessel sealer is used for dissection and bipolar cautery. As the dissection progresses towards the gastrojejunal anastomosis, the surgeon turns laterally to remove adhesions and better define the anatomy, continuing to divide the proximal Roux limb mesentery.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac, marking eight to ten centimeters from the pylorus to preserve part of the antrum. Posterior adhesions between the remnant stomach and pancreas are divided. The remnant stomach is divided using two fires of the Blucher Form-Sixty stapler. The lesser curvature is dissected for the passage of the second stapler, followed by dissection of the greater curvature. Posterior adhesions near the pancreas and splenic artery are sharply taken down. The fundus is dissected, and the gastrojejunal anastomosis is demarcated due to ischemic small bowel. The pouch is divided with a green load stapler after upsizing from a blue load. An endoscope is used to confirm the anatomy and size of the new pouch, ensuring all staple lines are contained within the specimen.

A 40-French bougie is inserted into the gastric pouch, and a green load stapler is used to vertically divide the remaining pouch. Indocyanine green is used to confirm good perfusion to the new pouch. The distal pouch, proximal Roux limb, and gastrointestinal anastomosis are freed from the left upper quadrant and set aside. The Roux limb is positioned adjacent to the pouch, and a two-layer hand-sewn gastrointestinal anastomosis is constructed. The outer layer is sewn with barbed monocryl, and the inner layer with dyed 2-0 Vicryl. The posterior outer layer secures the Roux limb to the gastric pouch. A gastrotomy is created to fit a 40-French bougie, and an enterotomy is made in the small bowel. The posterior inner layer is sutured first, starting on the small bowel side for forehand suturing.

The surgeon begins the anterior inner layer of the gastrointestinal anastomosis adjacent to the posterior inner layer using robotic suturing for precision. The sutures are placed but not tightened until a forty French bougie is passed through the anastomosis. The anterior outer layer is sutured halfway across using barbed monocryl, and the inner layer sutures are tied and cut. The anterior outer layer is completed, and the sutures are tied together to finish the anastomosis. A bowel clamp is placed across the rulum, and an endoscopic leak test is performed.
